{"step_by_step_thinking": "The child mortality rate is a key public health indicator that reflects the number of deaths of children under a certain age per 1,000 live births. Multiple documents in the retrieved sources specifically reference the age group used in child mortality statistics. For example, Document [5] (Pediatric Dehydration) states that diarrhea is the second most common cause of death among children younger than 5 years. Document [19] notes that in children under 5 years, diarrhea causes significant morbidity and mortality. Document [11] mentions that vitamin A deficiency affects children aged 5 or younger. Document [18] refers to pneumonia as a leading cause of morbidity and mortality in children younger than 5. These consistent references across multiple sources point to the age group of 0-5 years as the standard used in global child mortality rate calculations. The World Health Organization (WHO) and other global health bodies define child mortality as deaths of children under five years of age. Therefore, the age group used in the determination of child mortality rate is 0-5 years.", "answer_choice": "A"}
